clean_story:
Water in disguise. Life’s silent partner.

The word moisture comes from Latin musteus ("damp"), but it’s more than just wetness—it’s water hiding in air, soil, or skin, doing unseen work.

Moisture is the slow artist of change. A damp cloth left on a table doesn’t just dry; its water molecules escape one by one, like prisoners tunneling out of a cell. In bread, moisture keeps starch soft—until it flees, leaving crusts hard as armor. Too much, and metal rusts; too little, and wood cracks like old bones.

Plants are moisture’s best allies. A single oak tree pulls 40,000 gallons yearly from soil, releasing it as vapor—a living humidifier. Desert beetles do the opposite: their bumpy backs trap fog droplets, turning air into sips.

Three related subjects are dew point physics, how mold exploits dampness, and the body’s sweat economy.
